description: Duke Energy is a U.S. regulated electric and natural gas utility holding company serving customers across the Carolinas, Florida, Indiana, Ohio, Kentucky, and Tennessee.

overview.title: Business Profile
paragraphs: Duke Energy is one of the largest U.S. regulated utility holding companies, centered on electric generation, transmission, and distribution, with a complementary regulated natural gas distribution business. Its operating footprint spans fast-growing Southeastern markets as well as Midwest service territories, giving it the classic utility mix of essential infrastructure, regulated returns, and weather-sensitive demand.

The company’s core products are not consumer gadgets so much as civilization’s background process: electricity that should arrive without drama and natural gas delivery that mostly earns attention only when bills rise. That makes Duke Energy best evaluated through service territory, generation mix, rate cases, reliability, grid investment, and the pace at which fossil-heavy assets are replaced or retired.

overview.title: Electric Utility Operations
paragraphs: Duke Energy’s electric utilities serve millions of customers in North Carolina, South Carolina, Florida, Indiana, Ohio, and Kentucky. The business includes power generation assets, transmission and distribution networks, retail electricity service, and long-cycle capital projects overseen by state regulators.

For Free The World mapping, the relevant dependency surface is grid power itself: a regulated monopoly service where customer choice is constrained by geography, policy, and infrastructure economics. Alternatives are therefore less about swapping vendors overnight and more about distributed solar, storage, demand response, municipalization, community choice where available, and open grid modernization standards.

overview.title: Natural Gas Utilities
paragraphs: Duke Energy’s gas utility business serves customers in the Carolinas, Tennessee, Ohio, and Kentucky, including operations connected to its Piedmont Natural Gas acquisition. This business distributes gas to residential, commercial, and industrial customers through regulated local infrastructure.

The natural gas segment sits in the uncomfortable middle of the energy transition: marketed as reliable and comparatively lower-carbon than coal, while still locking in combustion infrastructure with long asset lives. Registry notes should track electrification pressure, methane emissions, rate recovery, and whether capital plans expand or sunset gas dependence.

overview.title: Transition And Infrastructure
paragraphs: Duke Energy frames its long-term strategy around cleaner generation, reliability, affordability, and major grid investment. Its transition themes include renewable generation, battery storage, nuclear generation, coal retirements, transmission upgrades, and resilience spending for storms and load growth.

The company’s public narrative emphasizes balancing decarbonization with reliability and customer cost, which is both a real engineering constraint and a familiar utility incantation. Structured research should distinguish measurable asset changes from broad transition language, especially around coal retirement schedules, gas buildout, storage deployment, and regulator-approved capital plans.
